text.title: Persona Building Process
text.summary: Five-step process for creating research-backed buyer personas: gather real data, cluster patterns, define segments, build persona with cited traits, validate with customers.

section.title: Data Sources

rule.statement: Conduct a minimum of 10 customer interviews before creating any persona. Do not build personas from imagination or market assumptions.
rule.rationale: Personas without interview data produce "demographic theatre" — plausible-looking characters that never change a real decision. Ten interviews is the minimum for identifying patterns that recur across multiple people.

section.p: Data sources ranked by signal strength: interviews (goals, frustrations, workflow), surveys (demographics, tool usage), analytics (behavior, engagement), support tickets (problems, exact language used), social media (interests, how they talk), sales calls (objections, motivations).

section.title: Pattern Clustering
section.p: Cluster interview findings on five dimensions: role/title, goals, frustrations, behaviors, buying triggers. Record both frequency (how many people said this) and intensity (how strongly they expressed it).

rule.statement: Every trait in the final persona must have a transcript citation in the format [transcript-id:line]. Traits with fewer than 3 supporting citations across transcripts are marked [WEAK] and excluded from the primary persona.
rule.rationale: Uncited traits are hypotheses, not findings. Marking weak traits prevents them from driving product decisions while keeping them visible for future validation.

section.title: Segment Types

rule.statement: Start with ONE primary persona. Add secondary personas only after the product has traction. Define a negative persona (who NOT to target) from the start.
rule.rationale: Multiple personas at seed stage split focus and produce muddled positioning. The negative persona is more decision-shaping than the primary — "who do we say no to?" is the harder and more valuable question.

section.p: Segment types: Primary (main buyer, most revenue — focus all design here), Secondary (important but smaller — support their needs), Negative (explicitly not for them — do not market to, politely decline in sales).

section.title: Persona Elements
section.p: Essential elements (required): name (memorable, role-based), role (job title/situation), demographics (only if 5+ transcripts agree), goals (what success looks like), frustrations (what blocks them), behaviors (how they work and buy), quote (verbatim from interview).
Advanced elements (add only when handing off to marketing or design): day in the life, tools used, information sources, objections, buying trigger.
section.reference: Full persona template with all fields

section.title: Validation

rule.statement: Show the persona to 3 real customers and ask "does this sound like you?" before committing the document to the repository. If 2 of 3 say "no", rebuild.
rule.rationale: Personas validated only internally often reflect team assumptions rather than customer reality. External validation catches the most common failure mode: the team's mental model of the customer diverges from the actual customer.

section.p: Update frequency: major update quarterly, minor tweaks as you learn, full rebuild after pivot or entry into a new market.
